text: Today I was craving stone crab claws, but before I dig in and enjoy these, let me show you how we got them. I went out in the boat, jumped in the water, and it was time to find some stone crabs. The first thing I came across on my dive was this coral head that was loaded with tons of small floor to spiny lobsters. While these aren't big enough to take home to eat, it is a good sign because you know that their population is growing. A little while after that, I came across a good sign. This right here isn't a dead stone crab, it's actually a stone crab mole. Because as they grow, they end up shedding their exoskeleton. Both lobsters and stone crabs do this, so it's common to see them if they're in the area. And it wasn't long before I came across the first stone crab of the day. When diving for stone crabs, the trick is to manage to reach in the hole quickly and get the crab out and manage to secure it before it manages to grab onto you. Because although the claws are delicious, they definitely pack a punch and you do not want these guys managing to pinch onto one of your fingers. It really hurts. After catching the stone crab, the next thing I do is I go ahead and measure the claw and make sure that it's big enough to keep. Here in the state of Florida, you don't actually keep the entire stone crab like a lot of other crab species. Instead, you actually just harvest the claws and then you go ahead and release the crab and it'll regrow its claw another day. To remove the claw, rather than forcefully breaking it off which can cause a life-threatening injury to the crab, I like to poke the membrane in its arm right here, which actually causes it to release it itself, which is one of its natural defense mechanisms. Then I just keep repeating the same process of catching the crabs, removing the claws, and then bringing them back down and letting them go in the exact same holes that I found them so that they can regrow them another day. Today, even came across a stone crab that was actually in the process of re-growing one of its claws. As you can see, it has one claw that looks at normal proportionate size, but then if you look on the other side, there's a teeny tiny new claw that's starting to form. And this is how you're able to have sustainable stone crabs for generations to come. But even though it's illegal for people to harvest the whole crab, there are other animals that definitely enjoy stone crab as well. If this octopus I found while diving had its way, it definitely would be eating a lot more than just a claw. Back inside, I brought some water to a boil and just added our stone crab claws in for the next five minutes. And after the five minutes was up, I just quickly removed the claws and added them to an ice bath. While I waited for those to chill, I just whipped up a classic stone crab mustard dipping sauce combining powdered mustard and mayonnaise, and then mixing in all the rest of the ingredients. And I actually find that the flavor of the sauce is even better if you let it sit in the fridge for a couple hours. Then when I'm ready to serve, I just go ahead and crack the crab claws. And just like that, we have delicious, fresh stone crab claws making the perfect appetizer. Stone crab season is something that I look forward to every single year. You just give it a nice dunk in the mustard sauce and enjoy. Mmm, does not get any more fresh than that. It's crazy to think that this same stone crab that we're enjoying right now is also alive and well in the ocean, and we might very well enjoy it in a couple years again. Now I'm going to finish eating the rest of these and until next time, get outside and see what you can find.
